Fyd sicrhau un b perché gweithio'r cyflosni yma fi r はrro o'r iawn. I'm going to share my screen and we are going to snowing. Not quite snow, Edward. It's probably 85 degrees outside here. And so... So... Let me see here. So... Yeah, you guys are so funny. All right. So we are going to now go into research and decisions. And so I just want to make sure as always, you guys can see my screen. A OK. Yes. Cool. Awesome. And so we're going to talk about research questions that you need to answer before you start writing and decisions to be made. Now, as I mentioned at the beginning of the the morning session, I'm not going to go into, excuse me, every single detail here as it relates to research and decisions. I felt the need to include this in here as well as some of the other foundational principles because so much of what we're going to be talking about tomorrow is based on stuff that we identify and uncover and get from this process. So keep in mind as I go through this that if there are specific questions that you have deeper than what it is that I'm covering here, if it's an urgent question, you could pop it into the little Q&A section. If not, hold it for Monday and I will answer it. I want to give you a strong, though, overview of the essentials of the research and and decision making here. And I'm going to start off with this and I say this time and time again. Every time I talk about, you know, research or examination, examination is step one of the or stage number one of the five method. Examinatio is the least enjoyable part of the campaign copywriting engineering process. It's the most tedious. It's the least fun, but it's where the gold is found at the end of the day. Most of what it is that we come up with in terms of ideas, hooks, themes, angles, pain points to to alleviate benefit statements to make come from the research phase, come from the examination phase. In other words, the reality is is that very little comes from within here for you. It's all out there. Grant, we're going to put stuff into your head. We're going to talk about developing ideas and all that kind of stuff in just a little bit. But it's all in the the research. It's all in really what you understand about the marketplace, the environment that we're about to that we're about to to go into or that we're going to be speaking to. You have to understand something. And this is really important that you're not talking to a blank canvas when you are talking to a prospect. In other words, they're not coming into your campaign as a blank canvas. They're coming into your campaign with existing fears, concerns, desires, ambitions, emotions, as well as beliefs, experiences, viewpoints and all of that stuff. Kind of it impacts the way that they receive and process your your message. And so you need to understand who you are talking to and what their perspective is, what they've seen, what they haven't seen, what they heard, what they haven't heard. It really all begins, as you heard me say already, with the prospect. And so this is critical, not fun, but critical, necessary, right? Like essential to what it is that you do. And I'll tell you that the best copywriters in the world were all and are all research fiends, research fanatics. The majority of their time, especially when they're writing campaigns for new products for things in a new marketplace, not their own stuff. The majority of their time is invested into the research and examination, right? Not the writing. Everything you need to know to be able to craft a compelling message starts with the research. And so with that, let's talk about the examination triad. And so I'm going to sum this up very quickly because this is covered in tremendous detail in the E5 method stuff here. That's a great way to put it, Lynn. Excuse me, the gold is elsewhere and we're just prospectors who know what gold looks like and how to shape it. You're absolutely right. That is so well said. And so we are just looking for it. It's out there. There are three things that we look at. There are three things that we examine and we examine them in this specific order. So the first one is prospects. And I'm going to break down and give you kind of a high level overview of exactly what it is that we're looking for when we are examining prospects. But we start with the prospects. These are this is your audience. This is who you are talking to in your in your marketing campaign. After we do the examination on prospects, then we go over to competitors. And we're going to talk about the two areas that we look at when we look at competitors, there are really two core things that we are going to use for the crafting of our of our copy. And then after we've examined prospects and then competitors, then we go over and we examine the product. Now, when I say examine the product, I'm talking about yes, even your own product, your writing for your own stuff, your own product, your own course, your own training, your own agency, whatever it may be, we're still going to go through the examination stage, as if you were a consultant brought in, as if you were a copyrighter brought in to write copy for somebody else's product. So just because it's your own, I don't want you to assume that you know everything that you need to know, that you're aware of everything that you need to be aware aware of, you still need to go through the full examination process for the product. So we do it in this order one, two, three. So let's talk about the prospects. So giving you this high level overview. So ultimately what it is that we are looking for, we're looking for number one problem identification. In other words, we want to identify the problem that they have or the problems that they have that we can solve. And we want to look at which is the most pressing problem, which is the most urgent problem, which is tied with the most amount of need, the most severity. And ideally, that's the one that we're going to go after. But we start by identifying what is the problem that we are going to solve. So what is the problem that the prospect has that they see that they have, that they're aware that they have, that they want to resolve on whatever level that we are going to, that we're going to solve for them. So you got to start by identifying the problem that they have, that they know they have, that we're going to solve for them in our copy. Next is we have to identify the their wants and desires. And what I mean by this in this particular context is as it relates to the problem, what do they want to see happen? What is the ideal solution that they're looking for? What is the outcome, the transformation, the result that they're that they're looking for? What do they want to be experiencing that they're not experiencing right now? What are they experiencing right now that they don't want to be experiencing after their problem is resolved? And so we have to identify or understand at least what are their their wants and their desires. We have to know this. You have to know this because right off the bat, you should see that. Right? How do you craft a compelling marketing message? How do you craft compelling copy if you don't know the problem that your prospect has that they want solve that you can solve? And how do you know what it is to say in your copy or what do you say in your copy? If you don't know the wants and desires that your prospect has at the end of the day, remember, you heard me say this, right? And I want to drive this home. I want you to get this at the end of the day. Ultimately, what we're doing is we're really again, we're telling them that we've got a different and superior way to solve their problem, to give them the wants and desires that they have so that they can feel the way that they want to feel. Right? That's ultimately what we're doing. And so unless we know what it is that the problem is, unless we know their wants and desires, unless we know the emotions that they currently have and ultimately what they want to have, well, how do we? How do we know that we're going to resonate with them? As it relates to emotions and feelings, there are two things that we're ultimately looking for here under this. So as it relates to the problem, what are the emotions and feelings that they can have? Right? Like what are the emotions and feelings that they have? And what I would tell you in this context, just to kind of go a little bit deeper here is don't get so caught up in how I mean the exact emotion. Is it anxiety? Is it disappointment or is it disappointment or is it frustration? There are so many gray areas in terms of in terms of emotions. There are so many so many emotions that that kind of cross over, that blending together. It's not this black and white emotional profile that your prospects have. Really, what I want you to to kind of come to is, is it a negative emotion? Like or is it more negative bent? Is it more fear based or is it more hope based? Is it more greed based? Is it more kind of positive? Right? What are the emotion? What's the emotion that they have coming into the campaign? So as it relates to the problem, are they is it an unfulfilled desire and they're hopeful, they're excited, they're ambitious, they're greed, you know, they're greedy, so to speak, or is it more of this unresolved distress where they're concerned, they're scared, they're anxious, they're frustrated, that sort of thing. And so I want to understand what the emotions are that they have. And like what's the core emotion? How would I sum up the core emotion that they're experiencing right now? Don't be so concerned with like, how do I know if it's again, disappointment or frustration? Like it's not about under, it's not about being able to name the exact emotion, it's about understanding the overall emotional state that they are in. And then, of course, you want to have an understanding of what are the emotions that they'll experience and that they want to experience after they experience the wants and desires. So when the problem is solved and alleviated and they're experiencing the benefits, the outcomes, the result, the transformation, the change, all that and all the ancillary benefits that come with that big overarching change. How are they going to feel? Right. And so we want to we want to understand that. Now, you can look at these three just right off the bat and you could start to recognize, of course, in what it is that I just said, that it's important to understand both sides of the equation so that we can present and provide contrast. Right. Like in other words, another way to think about this and this is something that I talk about in in E5 coaching is the before, the now and after state. So right now they're dealing with this, they're dealing with the problem and they're and dealing with their problem, their life looks a certain way. They're experiencing certain things. They're not experiencing certain things. What it is that they're experiencing or not experiencing is making them is making them feel a certain way. It's causing pain, certain pain points for them. Right. That's where they are right now. They have this overall emotional state about their about the now state. And then there is the after. Right. And the after is post problem being solved. So after the problem is being solved, after the problem is solved or relieved, now what does their life look like? What is what is part of their life? What's their what pain? Don't they have anymore? What pleasure do they have? All those things. And how do they now feel? Right. Er, excuse me, after how do they how do they feel? So we've got this now state and then we've got this after state. All right. And in the middle is the the problem being solved. That understanding that what it is that I just said is where the contrast comes from, the contrast comes from your dealing with this right right now. You no longer have to deal with this. You no longer have to experience X. You no longer have to be frustrated by by why? Because you'll experience element O pay all this good stuff. Darkness light, darkness light, darkness light contrast. This is where it comes from. And again, in order to convey that we've got the right solution for them, we need to understand these things. You also need to understand any potential beliefs that they have that could color the message, in other words, that contain the message that you give them. Remember, you're not dealing with a blind canvas. In some markets, in some instances, there are certain beliefs that the market has that is important to recognize. And I'm going to give you a couple of examples in just a second. You have to recognize, are there any beliefs that that if I immediately disagree with them, could cause them to view me as not be incredible? Are there any beliefs that if I contradict them early on, could cause them to to say he or she doesn't know me? They don't understand. They're not me. They're not one of me. They don't get me. They're not right. This is not like there's a there's a there's a disconnect here. Is there any belief that they have that if I contradict early on, could make them say wrong, absolutely wrong off the mark? And so let me give you an example. So one of my clients, one of my clients is the agor companies and I've worked with a number of of different affiliates over the years or different entities over the years. And and in the majority of the affiliates, the majority of folks, the the avatar, and I kind of hate using the term because of the connotation that comes with it, but the avatar, their ideal, their audience was really made up largely of 55 year old, 55 plus year old white men who are conservative politically. Right. They used to refer at Agora financial to their avatar as grandpa Kurt and these to have this little doll that represent who they were talking to. Well, you know right off the bat that they're talking to primarily conservative politically conservative people, people that right for the most part right wrong or indifferent, dislike the Clintons, dislike Obama, dislike Biden, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah. And so if we opened up a campaign, a financial campaign talking about how great, you know, the Clintons are what the Clintons are now making a move that's going to allow us to profit in a big way. And we're positioning the Clintons as great, you know, valuable, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah. Well, we're going to go against their belief. We're going to immediately butt up against this perception that they have that that's bad. I don't want anything to do it like if you tell me how to exploit their wealth, if you tell me how to get over on them, if you tell me how to how to capitalize on an opportunity because of their mistake, now I'm in. But if you try to align me with them, I'm out. Same thing with the alternative health space. The deeper you go into the alternative health space, the more belief there is again right wrong or indifferent that the pharmaceutical industry is evil, right, is evil. This is where it becomes like the common enemy, you know, like is evil is bad. And so if we say something early on, like this is, you know, this is thanks to the hard work that the people over at Johnson and Johnson did. Well, boom, they're immediately like, no, no, no, no, no, no, no, not good, not good. Those are bad. Those people are bad, right? And so, but if we if we opened up with more of, let's say, just as an example, the idea that here's an opportunity to, you know, to to to leverage, you know, the evil patents that Pfizer has to get the natural remedy that you need for gout or whatever, now they're interested, right, because they see those folks as as bad. Same thing, you could go into general, you know, into other markets and know that there are some kind of there are little beliefs that you just have to be aware of in some spaces like attorneys are shady, right, wrong or indifferent, right? Like that's some people have that, you know, belief. They don't necessarily think of attorneys and immediately think of the most ethical people right, wrong or indifferent. It's just this thing. Some people think of there are folks that think, you know, chiropractic and they think of chiropractor, they think of painful or risky or not a legitimate doctor or something like that. And so my point in sharing that with you is you just have to be aware of are their beliefs that I need to be aware of so that I don't contradict them. Are their beliefs that I that I need to make sure I don't contradict and are their beliefs that I can leverage that I can use to show the prospect, right, but that that we're the same, that we feel the same way without without coming out and saying we feel the same way. Are there beliefs that I need to be aware of that we can use in the campaign to show them I get you. We're on the same team. We feel the same way. We have the same worldview, right, because that's important. You have to understand that, you know, people like to spend money with people that that they like and trust and people like people that are like them. And so right, wrong or indifferent, right? People like people that are like them. And so, right, like depending on your stance on the vaccine, or nay. If somebody was. If you were yay and somebody was really outspoken and it came through outspoken against it, and they were you know, you may be a lot less inclined to buy and saying thing on the other side of the coin. And so my point is you're not dealing with a blank canvas. You've got to recognize who is the individual that I'm talking to. What are their wants and desires? Right? Yn ystod, mae'r prospect yn ei ddweud yw'r problem. Y prospect yn ymddi'r yw'r prospect yn ymddi'r cost yw'r lach o'r atention. Yn yw'r cyflwych, yw'r cyflwych ar y gwrdd ardal yn y copi, ychydig yw'r cyflwych ar gyfer y gallwn gwneud y problem. Yn ymddi'r gyflwych ar gyfer y problem, yw'r symptom, yw'r prospect. Okay. Rich identified an unaddressed root problem that he was the only one that had a solution to. So in other words when he said that your problem is not that you know is not figuring out how to do all these things, but your problem is that you're functioning as a as an opportunity seeker when the best of the best the smartest the most successful entrepreneurs the ones that aren't feeling the way you are are operating like strategic entrepreneurs and then he got by. He was a brilliant on that he sold the the prospect on that idea. Then in the end he offered them the opportunity to become a strategic entrepreneur to learn how to function as a strategic entrepreneur and back then he was the only one doing that. And he showed the marketplace which is why it did so well he showed the marketplace that right that the problem that they thought they had was it was just another symptom of the real underlying problem the real underlying problem. That he then was the only one that could solve and it did gangbusters right at work like gangbusters. That's the power of an unaddressed root problem when you can identify it you can't always identify it right, but you certainly can spend time looking at your prospects problem problems or the problem that you feel. You know you you are going to solve for them and then ask yourself right. Is there a reason or cause underneath this is there right is there a cause underneath this right and that that your solution resolves that we could then talk about that nobody else is talking about nobody else is addressing right so. While a lot of people talk about for example with productivity and they talk about having a time management system, a better way to do to do your to do list a better way to capture your projects right somebody could come along and say that the issue isn't your productivity system. The issue is your mental baggage about productivity and the reality is that until you identify and alleviate or remove your your mental baggage as it relates to productivity. You're going to try all the productivity systems in the world and you're still going to struggle. Fact is, once you eliminate your baggage your mental baggage related to productivity the reality is you could plug in almost any productivity system, and you're going to be much more effective with it. And so what I'm going to show you how to do is how to eliminate very specific beliefs that become or are your productivity baggage so that you can blah blah blah blah blah blah blah. Dw i ddechperforma drws rydw i hyn ar ddreol ar y sat Brain Louis ferfyrny'n gyda weld dim adran deddiol, Centuray jaen nhw mewn atyneth tyurtrgrno ddatigau ar gweud I want to know what not to say. I want to know right because I want to know what is the market already heard right so that I don't duplicate that I don't want to come into the market I don't want you to go into the market and I don't want you to say more of the same. You don't want to say more of the same you don't want to take the same idea the same hook the theme the same theme that somebody else is rolling with and you don't want to roll it out into. In your market and just try to say it better what I'm looking for is different remember different gets attention better doesn't always get attention. And so I need to know what are the hooks the themes the marketing ideas that competitors are using or even have used recently. So that I know what not to do. Then I want to understand what are their promises the claims the benefit statements that they are making. Now here yes, we can model what it is that competitors are saying. So I want to look for I want to look across the board at the folks that we're competing against, and I want to see what what outcome. Are they promising what result what transformation are they promising. What are the benefit claims that they're making right and I really want to look for trends and commonalities across the board. Right because a trend of commonality if I see multiple competitors making the same benefit claim promising the same kind of outcome the same kind of result. I'm going to note that I'm going to use that to my advantage. And so I want to understand. I want to look for these trends these commonalities with my competitors so that I can use them to my advantage. So hook theme marketing idea. We want to know that so we know what not to do promises claims benefits. We want to we want to know that and look for the trends so we know what to do. Next is, is there any kind of unique selling proposition that they're using likely not but I want to know it is their unique mechanism. What are they claiming is the way that their product or service works to produce the result. The majority of your competitors won't even have any kind of unique mechanism, but if they do, I want to know it right I want to understand it right because understand this. I've got to know the mechanisms that competitors are claiming so that when we develop a unique mechanism and I or identify our unique mechanism. I know that it's unique. If I don't know the mechanisms of competitors. How do I know if I have a unique mechanism or just a mechanism. Right like maybe it's a mechanism and it's a common mechanism because I don't know what my competitors have and they have the same thing. I need to know what what is it if anything that they are claiming is the reason why their product or service works. Next is, I want to understand their offer components. So what you could say here is that is that if we do this. Whoops is let's do this is do this. On second. There we go. Is that this is the marketing right and then this is the selling. So I want to know, I want to understand their marketing what they're doing in their in their marketing to position right so the hook themes ideas I want to know their promises claims benefits I want to know. Do they have a unique selling proposition. Do they have a unique mechanism. Remember, if we don't know what our competitors are doing, then how do we know how to position against that. How do we know how to differentiate if we don't know what competitors are saying. Right if we don't know right and what competitors are saying is ultimately what the marketplace is hearing right and so you heard me say I want to bring something new I want to bring something different. We're going to talk about that when we get to hooks and ideas in a bit, but right. I want to say something different I want to bring something different to the market I need to know what competitors are saying so that I know I've got something different. And then I want to understand their offer, because ultimately what we're going to try to do is we're going to try to put together a superior offer and offer that is better than competitors. Right and so I didn't say cheaper I said better. Right and so I need to know what it is that they are doing. And so that is competitors. Then with the product, we're going to look for very similar things. First of all, we're going to look to see is there any kind of catalyst, like we talked about earlier, that is that makes this product this approach this you know this system. This opportunity, more valuable, more timely, more urgent. Is there anything going on with this product in the marketplace that the prospects are aware of that I can leverage in my copy. Is there a catalyst, then I want to understand right we're going to and we're going to talk about this we're going to unpack these. I want to understand what's the hook theme marketing idea that we're going to roll with what are the promises claims benefits that we could make. What is there a unique selling proposition likely not but is there a unique benefit that we could offer. Is there a unique mechanism and then what's the offer going to be right these are things that we need to understand up front right we need to understand up front we need to make these decisions you heard me say at the beginning of this section. This is research and decisions. These are part of the decision I'm show you the questions that we need to answer. In just a second, these are decisions that we need to make before you write a single word of copy. Now I want to unpack a couple of these a little bit more. So you get the some of the nuances right I'm not going to fully unpack this I'm not going to go into every single detail. If or you could some of this carries over into marketing strategy but I want to give you the stuff that is important to know from a or for a copywriting perspective. So let's talk a little bit about. You're going to hear me use the phrase primary promise so oftentimes you hear me you will hear me use promise and benefits promise and benefits. I want you to think of promise as primary promise. And and and I want you to think of right primary we got two things we got primary promise and benefits. And so what's the difference because ultimately they're both they both could fall under the umbrella of benefits things that improve the prospects life that that alleviate their pain. Things done for with to the prospect, but I want you to recognize that every marketing campaign every piece of copy that you write in terms of a campaign has a primary promise primary promise is the chief result outcome or transformation. Meaning it is the one overarching change improvement result that your prospect is looking for. So the difference between the primary promise you need to understand this you're going to see this you're going to see this carried over to tomorrow. You need what you need to understand is that the primary promise is the big overarching change. But from that big overarching change and I'll give you an example in just a second. There will likely be a lot of smaller ancillary benefits that come with it. What I'm talking about here is what is the most valuable most compelling most sought after result that your prospect is looking to experience that your product or service can deliver. So if we were talking about weight loss if somebody was interested in losing, you know, X amount of weight 30 pounds of weight or whatever it is 30 pounds of fat. Well, right that that would be part of the big overarching primary promise. There's also lots of other benefits that they will experience when they lose 30 pounds right. They'll sleep better at night. They'll their cholesterol will come down better heart health right they'll gain more confidence. Maybe they'll the tendency to be more more social. They'll have more energy. They won't feel the five o'clock dip. They won't have as much knee pain. All of those are benefits. All of those are beneficial right all of those are things that we're going to talk about assuming that they're important to the prospect throughout our copy. But when it comes to the primary promise, you want to identify what is the one over overarching change transformation or result that we are going to focus on. The one big payoff that means the most to your prospect as it relates to the problem that they have. And when forming the primary promise number one is right is that promise of result when you're thinking about what am I going to promise them because in every marketing campaign right is built in some form or fashion on the back of a promise a promise of result. That promise of result needs to be what I call big, bold and audacious right it needs to be a big promise. It needs to be an audacious promise. It needs to be an exciting result. If it's not big. If it's not bold. If it's not exciting, then your prospect isn't going to care. In other words, if we were marketing and selling a traffic generation program right and we talked about our promise was how to how to get an additional 11 visitors a week to your website. Nothing else matters. Nothing else matters because the promise is insignificant right like it's insignificant it doesn't matter. You could have testimonials. You could have a great offer right all that but who cares about 11 more visitors a week. It's insignificant right to the average prospect. And so the promise the result itself needs to be big enough, bold enough and audacious enough. Next though it needs to be true and believable and true and believable are not the same thing. Right and so not everything that's true is believable and not everything that's believable is true. We certainly know that today. And so what you've got to recognize is that the big you want the biggest, boldest, most audacious promise of an outcome that you can make that is most compelling and most exciting to your prospect that is also true and believable. And for believable, what I would say to you is that you've got evidence support proof to back it up right to back it up. But we want the biggest, boldest, most audacious claim that we can make that is true and believable because we can back it up with support evidence for proof. And so big, bold, audacious, true and believable. Next, when you think of your promise and you heard me say this earlier and this is one of the most valuable lessons that I can I can share with you on this day and then you're going to see how this is played out in the actual copy tomorrow. But right a promise that is compelling is one that is specific, concrete and tangible. It's not vague. Vague promises of result vague promises of payoff mean nothing to prospects. They roll off your prospects back. It's lazy copy. It's the way the average marketer will will write there. They're either afraid to commit. They're afraid to narrow. Or they just don't spend the time in uncovering what the market really wants and what is the exact payoff that the market's craving that their product or service can deliver. But vague general claims, vague general promises are meaningless to your prospect. When you promise something, you want it to be specific. You want it to be concrete. You want it to be tangible. You want it to be something that they can see again in their minds eye. And a great judge of this, right, is that it's measurable. And what I mean by measurable is that they can know with certainty when it's been experienced. Like when it's been experienced. So you say to somebody, you know, um, you know, get a better night's sleep, right, get a better night's sleep. What does that mean? What do I, what do I picture in my mind's eye? Right. How do I measure that? What do I measure that based on, right? It's not specific enough. It's not concrete. It's not tangible. But if I said, right, sleep soundly that you'll wake up, you know, you'll wake up refreshed at the crack of dawn without ever needing an alarm clock. Right. Like now that's measurable. Right. Cause I can measure my actual experience against it. And it's that measurability that gives promises weight and impact. And that measurability only comes from specificity and concreteness specificity and concreteness. And so what you're looking for, right, is you are looking for a promise. You want to promise something that you can be specific. Better off like you heard me say earlier, you're better off narrowing it narrowing like shrinking what it is that you are promising shrinking, meaning narrowing the focus getting more more specific and being able to promise something specific rather than something vague. Right. So rather than you might be, you know, a lot of marketers are afraid to say like, you know, how to lose, you know, lose 13 pounds of fat from your waist in the next 21 days. And I'm just using that as an example. Right. What did I just say lose 13 pounds of fat in the next two weeks or 21 days from your waist. That is specific. A lot of marketers will say, well, I don't want to do that because there are a lot of people that I want to retract that they're not necessarily looking to lose just that amount of weight. They're not looking to just lose it from their waist. They're looking to lose a lot more. And so I just want to say something like reach your target, you know, lose enough weight to reach your target weight loss goal to reach your target weight loss goal. That's crap. That is garbage. Right. Because it's vague. It's general. You cannot picture it in your mind's eye. How quickly will I lose it? Where will I lose it from? How much will I lose? Right. Like that's garbage. That that type of vague generality should not be anywhere in your copy. And so what you want is measurable. I'm going to be able to know in the next 21 days whether I lost 13 pounds of fat like right like I'm going to be able to know that I'm going to be able to measure it. And so really important. Like I said, your prospect must be able to envision in their minds eye the promise that you are making. If they can't conjure it in their minds eye, you are not specific enough. You're not using concrete enough concrete language. And so the last thing that I'll say about the promise and then I'm going to answer some questions that look like they came in is is that these are two great exercises. I talk about this in E5. So I don't need to go crazy deep in here. But these are two exercises that you can do to help get an understanding of what your primary promise should be. Number one, the magic wand technique, right? If you could wave a magic wand and you could grant your prospects anything in terms of the problem that they're dealing with, the problem that you're addressing, based on what it is that you know about your prospects, based on the examination and and research, what is it that you would grant them? What is the result? What is the payoff? If you could wave a magic wand and there was one thing, right, you got to like, you can't say, you know, lose weight to improve your heart health to have more personality and blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, you got to narrow it down. What is that result? What would they tell you? What would they ask you for? Right? Like you, you could wave a magic wand and grant them one thing as it relates to the problem. Next is, excuse me, the ideal client exercise, right? Imagine your greatest future success story. Take a client that is your greatest success story. Like imagine this into the future, whether you've got a new business, not like, what is the greatest success story? What would you consider the greatest success story? And what exactly are the results that that client experienced? Right? What did they experience? What happened? What changed? What was the transformation? Right? What was the result? This is what this is where you and I want you to listen to me. This is where you've got to, and we're going to talk a little bit about that. I've got this somewhere in here today, I believe. Right? That you've got to sit back. You know, thinking time, thinking is one of the most valuable things that you can do for your business when it comes to marketing. Oftentimes sitting back, not in front of the computer, maybe with a yellow legal pad and a pen, and just sitting outside or sitting someplace that you don't normally sit. I have a chair over there in the office that I like to sit in. It looks out this window over here. And just thinking about knowing what I know about prospects, what do they want? Knowing what I know about prospects, what is their biggest concern right now? Knowing what I know about the prospects in the market, what's top of mind for them right now? What's going on in their head? What are they thinking? What are they feeling? What are they struggling with as it relates to what we do? If I could wave a magic wand and grant them anything right now, what would be the number one thing that they would ask for based on what it is that I know, what it is that I've seen, the conversations that are taking place on Facebook, based on the comments that I've seen on books related to this topic on Amazon, comments on blog posts, comments in Facebook groups, comments on YouTube videos. What is it that if I could wave a magic wand right now, what is it that they would ask for? If there was an ideal perfect success story that the market would say that's what I want, what would that look like? If I had to come up with, if there was one thing that I could promise them that they would say this is a game changer, this changes everything for me, this is exactly what I've been wishing for, hoping for, waiting for, what would that be? That kind of activity is not a one time thing. That is something that you should be doing on a regular basis. I talk about this with our top one clients. Many will be listening to this. I think there are a couple of handful, maybe only a couple on here right now. Is regular thinking time thinking, not thinking specifically marketing, knowing what I know and even it goes beyond, let me deviate for one second and say this. Even sitting back and thinking about knowing what it is that I know is out there that's being offered to the marketplace, knowing what it is that they're struggling with, knowing what it is that they want, knowing what it is that they don't want in terms of a solution, knowing what it is that they like about products, dislike about products. What could I offer? What isn't being offered? How isn't it being offered? If there was a change that I could make to a product or to a result or to an outcome, what would that be? What is a problem or an issue that's wreaking havoc for clients that nobody else is addressing, that I could address? Now I'm not saying that I ask all of these questions every time I sit down to think. I might take one and then just think about it for 30 minutes and I might come up with ideas and write down ideas. The majority of those, the ideas that I come up with in the thoughts might be just stream of consciousness. But the point is that is stepping into the market, seeing it from the market's view, your prospect's view, getting out of your own way, not simply saying like, man, what would be exciting to promote, not simply saying like, what could I talk really highly about? No, it's got to come from the market. It's got to come from the market. And so hope that makes sense. Before we go on to the next section, let me see. So there are some questions that came over. Okay, so let me see here. How detailed do we go down root cause symptom is no clicks, which is attention, which is really big idea issue, which is really lack of mechanism. I would say, so the question is, David asks, how deep do we go? Like how far down the root cause do we go? What I would say is you go down as far enough as, and he's talking about the unaddressed root problem, is you go down as far enough as necessary to hit a problem that is unaddressed that you can solve. And so, right? Like ideally, I'm going to go down like, let's say, right? It's really a big idea issue. Is anybody dealing with a big idea issue? Yeah. Okay, what's the call? No big idea because let's say there's no mechanism. Anybody dealing with that is that? No, boom, that's the one that I'm going to go for. So you go down as low enough, you know, you go down deep enough until you find an unaddressed root problem that you can solve. Once you found an unaddressed root problem that you can solve, there you go. You don't need to go any deeper. You only need to go deep enough, right? In some cases, you might not be able to. In other words, in some cases, you'll go all the way down and there's somebody that's addressing, right? There's somebody out there that's addressing all of those already. The point is that you only have to go down deep enough to identify an unaddressed root problem, even if there are below that, right? But if nobody is here, you don't need to go any deeper. And so, hope that answers the question. Yes, so what we're saying is have a way that is better and different to solve your symptom pain. Here's how. Correct. That's ultimately what we're saying. We're not necessarily saying it in those words, David, but that is absolutely correct. Simple. All you guys and gals, don't overthink it. Every campaign. I'm going to show you this. Of course, we're going to get into copy in words and you're going to see how here's a different way to get the result that you want. Here's a different way to solve your problem. Here's what makes this way better than the others, right? Here's how it works and here's why it works. Boom, boom, boom, boom, boom, boom. Get excited and this is something different from you've done before. And here's your chance to get it right now. It's not the mistake that, and I want to share this. The mistake is in thinking the novice mistake when it comes to copy is thinking that it's whizzbang words and phrases that mesmerize your prospect into buying. We're not using our words to trick the prospect. We're not using the words to try to hypnotically get the prospect to say, I want to buy, I want to buy. That's not what we're doing. That's nonsense. We're not using trickery. There needs to be strategy underneath what we're saying, right? And that strategy is ultimately that strategy is the high level view is what I'm giving you a chunk of today, right? That I've got something different and better to solve your problem and give you the result that you want. And here is why it's better and here is why it's superior and here's how you can get it. How we do that in the words and phrases and tonality and voice and language patterns and all that so that it bypasses their buying filter, their radar so that it doesn't come off obnoxious and pushy and aggressive and whatnot so that it moves people to be like, dang, this sounds amazing, right? I need to get this right now. That's what we're going to cover tomorrow. But the strategy is like, look, I got something different. This thing that's different to solve your problem and give you the result that you want is not only different, but it's better than everything else out there. Let me show you why. Let me show you how it works. Here is the proof. Here is the evidence. Here's what it will do for you instead of suffering with this. You could have this and here's how you can get it. That's it. Don't overthink it. So mechanism solves real problems and improving. What we're going to show is that we've got a unique mechanism, a different way to address that problem. Our different way is better. Our different way of addressing that problem is better. Here's why. Here's why. When you have an unaddressed root problem, you still have a mechanism, but you say our mechanism is more effective at alleviating these symptoms because it addresses the real underlying root cause. The only difference here, David, is that when you have an unaddressed root problem, the reason why the unique mechanism is so effective and so valuable is because it alleviates the unaddressed root problem. It is the only solution that addresses the unaddressed root problem. When you don't have an unaddressed root problem and you are just addressing a problem that others are, then we're going to demonstrate that our unique mechanism works better. I'm going to break this down for you in fact in just a second. Some level of superiority over the other options. I hope that makes sense. Todd, within the marketing world, a lot of people think they have a lead gen problem, but in reality it is an offer problem. Would that be a URP? I would say that you are likely right. There are a lot of people who think they have a lead gen problem. Most of the time it is not a lead gen problem. Most of the time it is an offer problem. But what I would say is that an offer problem or structuring the offer is not an unaddressed root problem. It is being addressed. There are many folks that talk about the offer. What I would say to you without going too deep, and this is something that we could unpack on Monday night, is there could be a part of the offer that people get wrong or do wrong or leave out. It is that part that is the cause of why their offer doesn't work. That is the trend that most markets follow. It goes from general to more and more specific. At one point in time, there was a town doctor. You went to that town doctor, whether you had a lump on your head, a gunshot or cancer. It splintered over time and specialty. At one point we saw with traffic generation, how to generate website traffic, paid traffic, Facebook traffic, Google ad, Facebook traffic, retargeting, and you get all this splintering going on. The offer fixing the offer, I wouldn't call it, again, I wouldn't say it's unaddressed because it is addressed. What you're looking for is there something underneath that that's unaddressed. What if I teach a thinking method so they can achieve their goals, any goal, how to be specific. I have many different success stories with great achievements with this method, but the achievements are as individuals can be. Just saying to somebody, I have a way for you to achieve your goals or follow this method and you'll achieve your goals. That's garbage. That's garbage because it has nothing to do with you. That's garbage because it's been said time and time again. There's zero differentiation there. There's zero separation there. There's very little belief and credibility there. There's very little, if anything, that I can picture in my mind's eye. What I would tell you is that, and this is something that you can do a little work and then you can share this with me, present this on Monday and I'll be more than happy to chat with you and unpack it even more, is what is a tangible result like that we can promise? Is it pick any one of your most important goals and I'll show you how to experience it in the next 36 days guaranteed? I want to get specific and that's narrow. You may be thinking, it's not just about one most important goal. It's about teaching people a way of thinking. It's about teaching people how to achieve their life dreams over the course, but you first have to solve the marketing issue, the belief issue, the credibility issue. We've got to solve that before we can help anybody. Before we can help anybody, they've got to buy. Before they could buy, we've got to create engagement. Before we create engagement, we've got to get attention. We've got to make sure that we've got something that's going to stop them, prevent mental opt-out, things that I'm going to talk about in a second. How else can we look at that? What else can we say? What is more specific? What is more concrete? How can we pick out a piece of that and even just take a piece of that whole process? Struggle how to make this measurable and less vague. Number one is, and some of this, this is so individual to every market that there is no one right answer. Obviously every marketplace is different. Again, we can spend a little bit of time talking about this on Monday. Brian, you're absolutely right. The idea of reduced stress is incredibly vague. What the heck does that mean? What the heck does that mean? Reduce stress. Same thing with less pain. What does that mean? Less pain where? How much less pain? When? Is it a little bit less pain? Is it a lot less pain? Is it pain free? Is it no more pain? Am I going to still need medication? Am I going to take medication? Is it so vague that it just rolls off? It is puffery. What I would say is, when it comes to stress, again, what are the symptoms that the prospect is having? What are they dealing with in their life? Don't just talk in vague generalities. In other words, they're stressed. What does that mean? What does that lead to? What does that cause in their life? What's happening? What are they feeling? What are they thinking? What are they seeing? Are they losing sleep at night? Have they lost their appetite? Do they always feel like they're on edge? Are they short with people? What is it that they are experiencing and dealing with? Is it causing them to drink at night? Same thing with the pain side. I'd be asking myself to say, what kind of pain am I talking about? Where am I talking about it? Is it stopping them from doing certain things? Are they struggling to get out of bed in the morning? Is it yoga for back pain that they get out of bed in the morning and they got to get out super slow because everything is stiff and tight and pain. They can't even tie their shoes so that we could say something along the lines of wake up in the morning, loose, limber, and be able to tie your shoes fully bent over without experiencing any pain or tightness. That's measurable. That's concrete. That's moving in the right direction. You just got to unpack a little bit more. You got to go from vague to specific. Stress, what does that mean in their life? What are they experiencing? What are they seeing? What are they feeling? How is it impacting them? What is the sign or symptom of this increased stress in their life? How is it manifesting itself? What is it about the way that it's manifesting itself that they don't want, that they don't like, that they're irritated by, that they're uncomfortable with, that we could say that will stop, that will no longer be there. You won't have to do that anymore. You won't have that anymore. You'll never have to take any kind of medication at night or supplement to fall asleep. You'll fall asleep within 15 minutes after you hit your head, hits the pillow. See how that's what we're moving towards more specific away from vague general descriptions. You're absolutely correct, Michael. This is all why. All this stuff that in here is why the examination stage is so freaking critical. It's why it is essential. It's why it is valuable. The more you know about your prospect, the more you understand about your market and your prospect, the easier the copy process becomes. The more you understand, the easier this process is. The less you understand, the more difficult it is. I would really say that a better way of saying it is the less you know about your market, the more likely you are to be off the market. That's really the reality because what happens is the less you know about the market, the more your own thinking colors your copy. Unless you are the market, you're likely to be wildly off the mark. Let's do this and let's keep cruising. Let's talk about the unique mechanism. Just a couple of things here to talk about the unique mechanism. Again, this is all really foundational stuff. The unique mechanism you guys know, hopefully, is the unique piece, part, component, process, aspect, framework, system, method, whatever you want to call it, formula behind your product which solves the problem and produces the result. It is how your product or service works. It's the reason why your product or service works. Whether you've got an agency and you do it for them, you're still producing a result for them. Remember, you're still presenting a solution. You're still solving a problem. You're still giving them the feelings that they want to have by giving them the result that they want by alleviating the problem. So, how, like, if you say up, we're able to get top search engine rankings, how, what's the process that you use and why is your process superior to every other agency, like why you is really why not, you know, why you meaning right like why your method. Why should I believe that your method is great. What makes your method special. What makes your process unique. And so, um, whoops, it looks like. Thank you. This is why I can't be left alone because there are no slides on the screen. How it works and the reason why your product works. And so this is the beauty of this, just to give you a little bit is you can identify this in any in almost any product or service. I always say all the time people ask when I'm on, you know when I'm when I speak conferences or these like episode podcast episode things. People will ask, like, who does, you know, does the does the five method work for everybody. And I always say the same thing that the only product or service that it won't really that it won't work for is when you are selling a pure commodity product. If you were selling a glass of top order, right then it's extremely difficult to develop a unique mechanism because it's a glass of top order it's a commodity that you can get the same glass of top order up the road to the right left, you know, north south, whatever. But as long as you're not selling a pure commodity, you can identify unique mechanism. The reality is, is that the unique mechanism is for lack of a better phrase right sharing with you guys in full transparency is a marketing invention. It's simply about looking at your product through a different lens. So while there are some products out there on the market that have what I would call again for lack of a better phrase, a legitimate unique mechanism, meaning they've got a proprietary piece of software they've got something patented. You know, they, they actually, they have an actual unique mechanism in there. The reality is, is that the overwhelming majority of unique mechanisms are marketing inventions they are. There are ways of positioning, repositioning and presenting a particular product or service. And so as long as you're not selling marketing and selling a pure commodity, you absolutely can identify a your own unique mechanism. The reality is this that the single most valuable thing that you can do for yourself to. And this is important right because tomorrow when we get into the nitty gritty details you're going to see me talking about unique mechanisms and how we present the unique mechanism and how we frame the unique mechanism and and and and what not. And that's why I want you to understand this. And this is essential this to me is one of the most valuable things for you to understand, as it relates to writing copy is the mechanism. The mechanism, the reality is is that the single most valuable activity that you can do as it relates to the unique mechanism is not naming it. Naming it is one of the easiest parts. It's also one of the most insignificant parts. I always say that there's only one rule I have between like only one rule I have for naming the unique mechanism that is don't name it something that sounds benefit driven. Don't name it something that sounds commercial don't name it the explosive muscle building system or the explosive muscle building formula, right, which clearly has no credibility and sounds commercialized. Instead what you want to name it. You want to name it something that sounds scientific something that sounds official something that could appear in a news article something that could appear in a college textbook. You want it to sound legitimate not like a commercialized benefit driven product save your product names for those benefit driven commercialized sounding fields but for the unique mechanism make it sound official right like and and the beauty is this I want you to understand something every single thing out there that has a name somebody named it. So one of the things that you know like we get clients that are like what do you mean that sounds kind of scammy and cheesy like scammy like I'm naming, you know I'm naming the mechanism like who am I to name it who was anybody to name anything. That's the reality. Who was anybody to name anything. Every single process system method framework out there that's got a name someone named it. Someone came up with a name for everything. And so the reality is is that why not you right and and you'll understand the foundation of why you should feel comfortable doing this in just a second but you name it you just don't want it to sound commercial you want it to sound more credible. That's that part that I just shared right there the naming it is the least important part of the entire process with the unique mechanism, the single most important part with the unique mechanism is that you spend the time interrogating your method, meaning that you spend the time unpacking. What it is that you do. Why does it work. How does it work. Why do you do what you do. Why those six steps. Why in that order. Why not seven steps. Why not the five steps. What happens if you do it out of order. Why does this work. When you do it this way. Why do you do it this way right all of that by spending that time on packing your mechanism. That's what's going to give you the information you need for something that we're going to do in just a little bit. That is the single most valuable thing that you can do with the unique mechanism is I is understanding how does your thing work. It's amazing to me gang how many marketers and entrepreneurs. I don't care agency folks. Educators trainers experts coaches consultants. I don't care. It amazes me how many people just take for granted the way their thing works it's almost like they they do it intuitively right and they've never spent the time to think about why do they do it that way and why do they do it in that order. And what would happen if they did it out of order. And what would happen if they added a step what would happen if they took out a step. Why do you do this first why do you do this second why do you do this third why are all these pieces necessary. What what role does this play. What if we took this out what would change what wouldn't happen what would that lead to all of those things right all of those things are part of the interrogation process to understand the the mechanism. And that's essential because that's where you identify what it is that we're ultimately going to use to give support the logical foundation the logical justification the logical support for what makes your mechanism superior. See check this out gang. I want you to listen to me for for one second here. So and then somebody remind me that I got to share my screen again once I'm ready to to share to share the screen. So check this out right so you know you know we talked about we talked about like look you're ultimately showing your prospects your marketplace that that you've got something different and superior that your solution listen to me that your solution is the best right that's ultimately what we're saying that your solution is superior. Well for every single one of you, I have to believe that you believe that your solution is the best. I have to believe that you're doing it the way that you're doing it whether agency, whether the system that you're teaching or equipping people with or showing people. I have to believe that you believe it is the best. I have to believe that if you didn't think it was the best, you would do it a different way. Right you're not intentionally doing or using a sub par method. I believe that you believe you are doing it because you believe it's the best. Therefore, right therefore we just need to understand why do you believe that it is the best. Why you believe that it is the best comes from the interrogation. It comes from the questioning of why do you do what you do. Why do you do it this way. Why not do it a different way. What would happen if you changed the order of it. What would happen if you took a piece out what would happen if you put something random random in what would happen if you skip this step what would happen if you did this step a different way. What would happen if you automated it instead of doing it the manual way that you do it. Why do you do it the manual way. What's the benefit of that. What's the change of that. See because of all of that. All of the questioning of that gives you the reasons why the reason why we do this is because x, y, z. It leads to element. Why is yours the best. Why is your superior. Why is your method superior to every other method, not why are you superior. ysplol? This new thing, this different thing, right? Is better than everything else. So we're not just giving them, we're not just giving them something better. We're giving them something different, which is better, right? So we're not just improving on or fixing some small little piece. We're giving them something different, which is superior. I'm going to show you one improvement on how on how to do that one thing that's going to change everything for you.
