And so, I want to talk about the offer, and I want to talk about just one piece of the offer and this is something that's really been on my mind, a lot more lately, something that I've communicated with my team, a lot more. I mentioned this earlier that there is a neglected component when it comes to putting together offers that in the spirit of transparency, I only really learned. I shouldn't say it only really became top of mind for me. And really I would probably say it became really top of mind for me in like the last 18 months or or so. And so I want you to think of the the offer, and I want you to think of there being two main kind of elements to main buckets or categories. When we think of the offer and the reason why I what I started to say was that, like, this is, this is money, you know the offer is so important like we talked about at the very beginning of the morning workshop the offer is a make or break element. And this one part that we're going to talk about is really a make or break element in in terms of the offer and it's something that most marketers don't know and or neglect. So two buckets, the first bucket that people that people think of that most people go to that I used to go to, when it comes to offer creation is the idea of an irresistible proposition. And the irresistible proposition is this idea that is it's really made up of those elements that we talked about earlier what are the deliverables what's the features the advantages the benefits of those things the functional dimensionalized emotional. What's the price what's the terms what's the risk reversal what's the premiums what's the reason to respond now. And when most people think about, and listen to me when most people think about putting together an irresistible offer what they're really thinking about is the irresistible proposition portion of the offer. They're thinking about what deliverables can we add. What are the right deliverables. What deliverables would be extremely valuable. What are the benefits that we can articulate with all these deliverables. What's the right price point. What's the right terms. Should I go with a soft offer should I go with a hard offer. What's the right risk reversal should we go conditional unconditional should we use a combination should we give double your money back. Right what are the premiums that we're going to add in what are the premiums address what additional benefits they give right and what they think of when they think of putting together an irresistible offer is they're really thinking about this component, the irresistible proposition. And while this is important. What I said earlier to you guys and gals is even more important, and that is the other component being what I refer to as an attractive solution, an attractive solution is ultimately how compelling how interesting how attractive is the solution that you are proposing the prospect pay for right so is it is it an attractive solution. And you're making an irresistible proposition for an attractive solution, or is it an ugly solution, and yet you're still making an irresistible proposition for that ugly solution, because it's these two things combined that ultimately determine how, how compelling your offer is how good your offer is, and you need both. And this is the thing that right like I really didn't fully grasp or I wasn't able to wrap my head around, I was almost in the spirit of transparency. I almost used to think that it was, you know, to give an extreme example that it was a matter of the product, the solution is the solution is the solution is the solution is the solution, it is what it is. We're not going to look to improve it to make it more attractive, whatever whether we're selling something gorgeous or selling something hideous it is what it is. Right. And we're just going to make the most irresistible proposition that we can for this, this hideous thing, right, let's say right and I almost didn't put any thought into from a marketing perspective how attractive the solution was, I just put all the thought into making an irresistible proposition. The reality is, is that a really hideous solution that I don't care how irresistible the proposition is people aren't going to want it. And so, and so what I'm saying is right. If something is or feels extremely difficult to use. It takes a long time to see the rewards of. There's a steep learning curve it requires a massive investment. It's tedious. All that right if you've got an unattractive solution, an irresistible proposition might increase the amount of people that say yes, but it's not going to push you to that double digit sales conversion rate that that folks are after. And so you need a combination of both. And so I just want to quickly cover the elements of an attractive solution so I'm not going to talk about the irresistible proposition I've talked about that many, many times before. I want to talk about what makes for an attractive solution. So right if you are let's go back to that weird example of the I want to wash green lawn right and and so I've got a system that I'm going to teach you that I'm going to equip you with to to get that wash green lawn. You follow this system and it's going to give you a wash green lawn, you know gorgeous full blah blah blah lawn is going to look like carpet or whatever. You know, in, you know, in five weeks or whatever. And I'm going to teach it to you I'm going to teach it to you with, you know, videos and cheat sheets and blah blah blah I'm going to give you all this stuff and here's what it's going to do for you and and here's the price and here's the terms and here's the guarantee and and and blah blah blah blah blah blah. Well, as I said earlier, if that method for growing a green lush lawn is extremely tedious if it requires four hours a day of my time, it requires manual labor me out there working with tools that I then have to invest in out in the out in the sun. It's a very precise process or very precise solution so if I make one mistake. I blow the whole thing. It works very slowly so I'm not going to see the first sprout of, you know, of grass for for weeks. What was I going to say, and you know, and because it's so tedious and so in depth and requires consistency, there is a low likelihood of success. I don't care how irresistible the proposition is, you're not going to get the masses to take advantage of that, because the solution itself isn't attractive. Remember when we talked about the offer we said that the what is the prospect need to give, and what do they get what do they need to give in time energy effort manpower resources investment as well as the money exchange to get the actual solution to get the product and so. So, I want you to understand that our aim when possible, and I want you to think about this in the context of creating new product new offers going forward is that is that the beauty in something like the. In the agency consulting coaching courses training products information products live events master all that kind of stuff. I want you to think that you've got the ability to create new solutions to re engineer solutions to make them more attractive. And what we ultimately want to do is we want to start with engineering creating an attractive solution, and then wrap it in an irresistible proposition. So think about like how attractive is the is the solution that we are offering in the deliverables as part of the proposition, we want to start with making our solution as attractive as possible, and then we want to wrap it in an irresistible proposition. So I already said, and this is right wrong or indifferent right this is just the reality of of what exists in a powerful solution or in an attractive solution. Number one, it solves a significant problem that goes without saying right so it's only valuable based on the problem that it it it solves. It produces a big desirable result. And so remember I said earlier that it doesn't. We're not talking about insignificant results. The bigger the result, the outcome the transformation that a solution produces, the more attractive it is, the smaller the result, the outcome, the less attractive it is right so you'll get 11 extra visitors a week to your website using my traffic system right is not attractive. Even despite the rest of these elements here, if that is a tiny result, a tiny outcome. It doesn't make for a very attractive solution and therefore that unattractive solution wrapped in a new resistible proposition isn't going to convert nearly as well. And so I want something that produces a big desirable result. Next is, it's new. It's not simply better. And this I already alluded alluded to earlier. This is replacement, not improvement. That's something that Dan Kennedy wisely said many, many years ago. My buddy Russell Brunson co founder of ClickFunnels talked about in one of his books, I think. And that is that we're giving them something new. We're not giving them an improvement to what it is that they're already doing. We're giving them something new. We're replacing the old. We're getting rid of the old and replacing it with something new. We're not taking the old and making it better. We're replacing the old with something new. And so if you think about, you know, the look at the E5 method as an example, the E5 method. For example, the E5 method isn't a way of doing better marketing. It is a system that replaces your old method of marketing. It's a system that replaces the old approach or the other approaches to crafting a customer acquisition campaign. And it replaces it with an altogether new system. And so the solution, it becomes more attractive when it is new, not simply better. When it's new, right? When it's different, new and obviously superior, which we're going to talk about or I'm going to mention, you're going to see here, which I've said umpteen times already. And so replacement, not improvement. It sounds strange when you think about like, you know, when, when I first started pondering this many, many moons ago, when I first heard Dan say this, I was like, is that really true? Like don't people really just want a slight tweak, a slight change to the old to make it better? Like it doesn't replacement seem, you know, seem, you know, more like more work involved. But the reality is, is that replacement gives people hope because prospects doubt, especially when they've tried something for a long period of time when you're dealing with prospects that have tried the old way or the previous way with lack of results, right? They doubt the likelihood of a small tweak. They doubt the reality of a small tweak being the thing that changes everything. And so they doubt that, right? Like how could it be one tweak to what it is that I've already done that's going to produce these big, gigantic results when I haven't had any results prior? And so the belief in the viability of the thing increases when it is replacement, not improvement. Next is, of course, I said, it's viewed as unique and different. So we're not selling more of the same. We're not selling an improvement on something that's already out there or a tweak or an enhanced version of what's already out there. We're selling something different, right? Something unique. Next is, it's seen as superior to other options. We talked about this already. Next is, right, wrong or indifferent, it's quick and easy to implement. The reality is, is that not everything is quick and easy to implement. Not everything can be presented as quick and easy to implement. For example, I never use the, I never use that phraseology when talking about the E5 method, that it's quick and easy to implement because it's not quick and easy to implement. But fortunately, it produces better results than anything else. And so the reality is you're not always able to have a solution that is quick and easy to implement. But the quicker and easier something is to implement, the more attractive it becomes. You should know that with people in general. They want quick, they want easy, they want simple, and they want fast results, right? They want fast results. And I would say that the reality is, is that those two bullets right there are probably the single two biggest elements of what make a solution attractive. The easier and quicker something is to implement and the faster the results that it produces, the more attractive the solution is. The more attractive the solution is. This is why when it comes to quick and easy to implement, this is why in the training coaching consulting world, the more that something can be positioned as done for you, or done with you, the more of the work that can be done for them. The closer that you can get to done for you, the more attractive the solution becomes, right? That's the reality, right? Wrong or indifferent. And the reason why is because faith and belief in the result, in the confidence in the outcome increases, right? It increases because the more things that they have to do themselves, the more likely they are to screw it up. The more steps there are to something that I have to do on my own, the greater the likelihood of me screwing something up. The less that I have to do, and the more that's done for me by the pros, by the experts, by the creators, whatever, then the more confidence I have in being able to get the result. The more attractive the solution looks to me. When you've got something that solves a big problem, a significant problem in my life that I want resolved, and it produces a big result, a big desirable outcome transformation, and it does it in a way that is new, is a replacement to the old, right? Something that I see as it's unique, it's different from anything that I've tried before. I believe that it is superior in producing results, and it produces quick and easy results, and it produces fast results. Then there's a portion which at least can be positioned as done for you, or is done for me, so to speak, right? And I have a high level of success. You have an extremely attractive solution. When you take that extremely attractive solution, and then you wrap it in an irresistible proposition by, again, like making the terms, a no guarantee risk reversal, the premiums to fill in any gaps, you've got something that people leave back. And so this is the kind of stuff, I'm sharing this with you here, because I just want you to understand the thinking, the mindset, right? And so the beauty in the agency world, the beauty in the coaching, consulting, information product, course creation world is that we get to sit back and we get to engineer and re-engineer solutions all the time. And the reality is from a marketing perspective is you want a solution that is as attractive as possible. I'll also tell you that the more attractive the solution is, the higher the price point can be. The less attractive, the lower the price point has to be, period, end of the story, right? And so the more attractive the solution, the more valuable the solution, the more attractive solution the easier the conversion process is. A more attractive solution will even make up for weaknesses in the proposition, right? And when you combine an attractive solution with an irresistible proposition, you've got the best of both worlds. Now I'll tell you just to put a final kind of bow on this, right? I told you guys earlier that at the beginning of our day that the single best thing, the single most valuable use of your time really is in offer creation. I'll tell you that this is where I would put the lion's share of my effort when it comes to new offers is I would engineer, I would look at, I would identify through ongoing examination, I would identify problems, significant problems that the market has that I believe that we can solve. Then what I would do is I would create an engineer attractive, an attractive solution, as a track, I would hit as many of these as I possibly could. And then I would take that, and then I would wrap it in an irresistible proposition. I would form or create or engineer the most irresistible no brainer proposition that I possibly can that would make it harder for the prospect to say no than to say yes, like I would make it that good that much of a no brainer. And then starting with that, starting with that bad boy, then I would go and I would engineer the copy because the job now of the copy is so much easier, thanks to how good the offer, the offer is. And so that is the offer solution. Now, I want to talk about decisions and making decisions. And I want to give you kind of the streamlined approach, and then the more in depth approach to these are decisions that need to be made before you are before you can write any, any copy. And so number one, what I call the bare bones big three, based on the examination so based on prospects competitors and the product based on our examination based on what it is that we now know based on what it is that I shared with you at a bare minimum bare bones there are three things that I need to know at the very beginning. Before I start writing any copy number one, what is the targeted problem that I'm going to address. What is that problem. What is the pain associated with it. And so that's number one bare minimum, you need to know that number two is what is the primary promise. What's that big overarching outcome of result transformation that I'm going to that I'm going to present with specific concrete tangible measurable language, and then three is process for the unique mechanism. So I did this just the these three P's just for you to remember it but think of the problem, the promise, and the process being the mechanism. And so what is the mechanism by which I am going to fulfill that promise and alleviate that problem. These are the bare bones big three that you need to know that you need to be clear on at the very beginning right what's the problem, the pain associated with it. What's the promise, the outcome, right the result, the transformation, and what's the process that we're going to give them, or use with them to deliver that promise and alleviate that problem. Those are the big three. You want to dive deeper into this here are the questions that we answer at the at the beginning, and you've heard me say this in some form or fashion, a number of different ways today already. What's the problem that you're going to solve. Number two, what are the core benefits that you're going to focus on. So what are the core, you know the core results, the core outcomes, the core changes, the core improvements to the prospects life based on what it is that we know about the process. What are the core aspects, right what are we going to focus on. What are the core emotions that we're going to prod, which we're going to talk about, right what are the emotions, the buying emotions that we're going to push that we're going to prod throughout are we going to use, you know, fear of missing out or we're going to use, you know, exclusivity, or a sense of belonging. What are the emotions that we're going to prod, which we're going to, we're going to talk about is it, or we're going to talk about greed, all that. What's the idea hook that we're going to use. And so what's the theme, the idea behind the campaign. What's the unique mechanism. What are the objections concerns beliefs which I need to address. Right so what are what are the beliefs the concerns and the objections that I need to address. Now this is a big this is a strategic thing here like this is how you identify this is less of a copy thing and more of a strategic thing. What I want to understand is this, that the aim of our copy is really to override the misbeliefs, the objections, the concerns, and to replace them with what it is that we need the prospect to believe. Right, in other words, we're going to, we're going to correct the misbeliefs we're going to correct or eliminate the objections and replace them with the beliefs that we need the prospect to have to want to buy. I want to unpack this for you tomorrow and give you examples of how, how I use copy to overcome certain misbeliefs or certain objections or certain concerns to lead the prospect to see that our solution is the smartest one for them. What's the vision that I'm going to create for the after state. So when I said like, what does life look like for your prospect after they after they solve the problem after they're using your met like what is life look like after the problem is solved with your solution with your mechanism with your framework with your approach with your methodology with your formula, whatever it is, right, what is that after state. Remember, we are the director of the movie in the mind of the prospect we're creating mental images pictures what are those mental images of what life can look like after the problem is solved thanks to our solution. And so what is that vision what does that look like that also comes from that examination. What does the prospect want the prospect is here they want to go here what does here look like. What are they hearing what are they seeing what are they tasting what are they touching right all that. What does their life look like after because that's the vision that we are going to create for them and we're going to tie that vision to solving the problem with our solution. So what is that vision. What's the prospects core emotion about the problem we already said, I already talked to you guys about what what is that general emotional state of the prospect coming into the campaign. So based on the type of problem which bucket is it, you know, an unresolved, you know, is it unresolved distress. Desire, what is the core emotion that the prospect has coming in. Are they excited. Are they stoked, or are they dejected, are they is it negative, you know, and this is an important thing like I want you to understand. So, so check this out. So I want you to understand the the this one question for one second before we go on. So, you know, I've seen this mistake where, you know, there are a lot of different types of campaigns, a lot of different lead types. You know, like, they're certainly like, you know, there's fear based a fear based campaign, for example. Excuse me and what I've seen is I've seen marketers, I've seen entrepreneurs that will they'll see a campaign out there in the world out in the wild that worked really well for a particular product or service. And let's say it's fear based, right, like the end of America, right, and they, they think, wow, like, man, that is, you know, that thing worked like gangbusters. Let me take that fear based promotion, that fear based approach. And let me use it in my world, and they take that fear based approach, and they bring it into their world. And let's say as an example, they end up with some fear based promotion about learning to play piano. Like, what? Huh? You know what I mean, like, where did that even come from? Right, like, see, that is, that is a disconnect with how the prospect feels. Why would you use a fear based promotion when somebody is interested in learning piano, when something is an unfulfilled desire and they're coming in in a positive excited mental state and they're looking forward to this new hobby and they're looking forward to learning this new skill and showing off. And all of a sudden, you're like, you know, you know, like, you're, you know, the whole, the whole future, your future happiness is about to fall apart if you don't learn how to, how to play piano like what, what in the world are you doing? That's, and I see that more than you could believe, I see that, right, and that is a misunderstanding of the core emotion that your market has coming in, right? You don't try to use a fear based promotion unless you're talking to people that are, that already have fear about the topic at hand, right? Like you don't use a fear based, you don't start off negative when you're talking to people that are coming in positive. You don't talk, you don't start off positive when people are coming in fear based. You know what I mean? Like you don't start off all happy go lucky if you're talking to people that are terrified that their new diabetes diagnosis is going to ruin their life, right? So, you don't start off, you got to understand the core emotion that people have coming in. And so what is their general emotional state coming in? Next is what is the buying belief that we need to establish in the mind of the prospect before going into the offer? I want to break this down for you in another two slides because this is critically important. I always start out and I want you to start out before you write any copy knowing where you are going to lead your prospects. What is the one belief that you are going to establish in their mind, the aim of your marketing, the aim of your body copy, the aim of your argument, right? What is the one belief that you need prospects to have before you go into your offer so they jump at your offer? So they see it as the smart decision, right? See, understand and this is something that we'll talk about in just a little bit. I told you this already. Everything that I do and say and show and present and demonstrate is done and there for a reason. There is a strategic purpose behind everything that I do. I start off understanding these things. What is the problem that we're going to solve? What are the benefits that we're going to focus on? What are the core emotions that I'm going to prod? And everything that I do fills in one or more of these, right? So everything that I say is either in there to demonstrate how we're going to solve the problem, to demonstrate how it alleviates the fear, to demonstrate the outcome that they can expect, the positive outcome, the benefit, to prod and emotion, to show what makes this different, to show what makes this superior, right? Like everything that is done is done for a purpose and you're going to understand in just a couple of slides how all of that can be summed up in one belief so that ultimately everything that we do and say and show in the copy is there to lead your prospects to that one belief. And so, right, that I call it the buying belief and we engineer that like we formulate what's called a campaign thesis, which we'll do in a second. This is, you know, just a simple list of and this is right like there are more emotions than this, but at the end of the day, right, this is like what are the emotions that you're ultimately going to prod throughout your campaign. I want to just talk a little bit about this for one second that like this is another thing it goes back to, you've got to understand people overthink and I want you to listen to me here. People overthink this emotion, the emotional stuff as it relates to the kind of the buying emotions, if you will, they overthink it and they get way too in the, in the, in the, like they get lost in here. What I want you to think about is as it relates to your product, your solution, and the transformation, the alleviation of the problem, the alleviation of the pain, and the result, the after state that it produces, what are the emotions that are tied to that. Right. And so what are the emotions that are tied to that like is if you're teaching people piano, let's say, is anger anywhere in that realm. Any aspect of learning to play piano that that that is tied to anger in any way can we leverage anger to help sell more piano lessons. Right. I would have to say no, likely not unless we're talking to individuals that, you know, got swindled by, you know, some prior, you know, a piano teacher, something like that. The answer is no, if we're talking to let's say, if we're talking to, let's say, you know, the, the grandpa Kurtz, the 55 plus white men conservative, right, and this is the Democrats are trying to keep you from profiting from, you know, from the most valuable investments on Wall Street. Here's how to. Here's how to strike back. Right now you've got, you know, like you've got anger, right, which is they're trying to keep you from, you know, from, from benefit from benefiting. Same thing with if there was like the alternative hell space anger towards the pharmaceutical industry. And so can we leverage that in there. Like, like can we leverage that anger will some anger may exist if they believe that we can demonstrate that they're trying to keep you from profiting from. And so we're trying to keep you from the most effective natural remedies because it doesn't help them to, to, you know, to make money. And so right so in that see how it's more of a natural, a natural fit is there let's say in, in piano lessons, is there any, any, what was I going to say, is there any tie to guilt and shame. You know, likely not. And like, look, my point is that there are some emotions that are just natural fits, right, like there are some emotions that are natural, that are natural fits. You know, there might be, let's say, in business development, like in business growth, you know, growth, right, I know that I can prod greed. I know I can pray. I know I can prod belonging and exclusivity. I know I can prod pride. I know I can prod envy. Those are all emotions that I can prod in there. I probably could tap into anger, if I wanted to, or fear, if I wanted to. I personally don't like tapping into fear and anger. It's just not my style. It's not what I want to, you know, put out into the world, but I certainly could. But I know that right greed fits belonging exclusivity fits pride fits and envy fits. Right. And so I'm going to leverage those. But if those aren't natural fits, don't try to make them fit. Right. That's the same mistake of the piano, you know, program, trying to like, how can I do a fear bros based promotion. Right. Like, why would you do a fear based promotion for something that has nothing to do with fear? Why would you do an anger based promotion that has nothing to do with anger where there's no anger that exists? Why would you try to do a, why would you try to leverage guilt and shame when you're selling piano lessons or like, you know, like you're selling a fishing lure? Are you going to, you're going to sell it based on guilt and shame? You know what I mean? Like you might leverage a little bit, a little bit of, you know, of like, don't be embarrassed in front of your friends as they're landing the big bass and you're coming home with nothing. You know what I mean? You know what I mean? Um, kind of thing. But you know the, the, the point is that look for the natural fits. Don't try to force it. That's what a lot of ridiculous copy. When I say ridiculous, I just mean unbelievable copy that doesn't have an impact because it is, um, it's, it's a stretch. It's, it's overzealous is copy that tends to or tries to leverage emotions that are just not the right fit. And they make leaps from like, from what their product does to benefits to certain benefits. Like a couple of weeks ago on a couple of weeks ago on the our team marketing call. And I'm not sure if, if Chris is on here. So Chris Cole is a member of our team. And so Chris was on this call. We looked at a promotion for a graphic design piece of software, like a graphic design piece of software was a new, new software graphic design. And it was so outlandish because this guy was saying, right with this graphic design software, you will get endless sales or something like that. I don't remember the exact verbiage. Um, um, but it was like, right? This, you know, there was a new, a new way of creating graphics with this grant all in one graphic design app or something like that. And this guy was tying to this all in one graphics app, this idea of finally, like grow your business rapidly, get endless traffic and endless buyers, you know, like finally generate the sales that you deserve. How the heck did you make a leap from a graphic design software all the way to sales? That is an example, which we're going to talk about tomorrow of overreaching. Overreaching. That is, there was a question asked earlier about, about, like how, how deep do you go? You know, like how, you know, and, and, you know, at one point on one of our calls, Teddy Garcia, our chief technology office, he even asked like, well, when do you stop peeling back the onion? Like when do you, you know, cause you could say you graphic design, you know, you create better ads and what does that do for you? That gets more attention. What does that do for you? It gets more clicks. What does that do for you? You know, it gets, you know, it drives more traffic in your website. And what does that do for you? Well, it could get you more leads and it could get you more sales. It could get you like, where do you, where do you draw the line? You know, you draw the line when we're going to, again, we're going to talk about this tomorrow. You draw the line when there is other stuff that plays a role. Like, you know what, more attractive ads, like, like, look, you can't say that more attractive ads are going to get you more leads, right? Because maybe you've got a crap opt-in page. You can't say that simply having more nice graphics in your ads are going to, are going to give you endless sales. It's ridiculous. It's a ridiculous reach. It's a ridiculous stretch. Right? It's a, you're leaping from one thing all the way to something else. Right? Like that's absolutely ridiculous. And I see that time and time again with emotions being prodded. Right? And thinking that, like the more of these emotions that I can use, the better. That's true when they are appropriate. When they are appropriate. When they are appropriate. And I'll tell you just to make a note of this thing that fear is the most powerful emotion on this list. So fear is the most powerful emotion on this list. However, what I will tell you is this, that fear based promotions tend to be either boom or bust. Right? They either work really well or they do not work at all. Fear based promotions are kind of be extreme pop, not my style. Right? And, you know, I'm willing to sacrifice the result that we can get from fear based promotions because I just don't want to add that. Like that's not, I just don't want to do that. But the reality is, is that they tend to be boom or bust. Very similar to, by the way, just for what it's worth is, and we're going to talk a little bit tomorrow about storytelling and the right way to do it and how to avoid a bold story and blah, blah, blah. At all this, like some cool tricks. But the reality is, is that, you know, and this is something for you guys to just grasp and write down. You know, there are a lot of people talk about, you know, rightly so. They talk about the power of storytelling. They talk about the story in marketing and they talk about the power of the story based lead or story based promotions and how they can be wildly effective and they can be wildly effective. Absolutely. When done right, they can be wildly effective. The reality is, though, is that similar to fear based promotions, story based promotions also tend to be either boom or bust. Meaning that when they're done right and it's dialed, they can do extremely well. But when it's off the mark, even just a little bit, it's a dud. And so this is why I tend to not use story based promotions. Is that because they're not effective? Oh, they're wildly effective. It's the odds. Right. The reality is, is that the odds of it being spot on. Like it's the hardest type of campaign to create and create well, I should say, right. It's the hardest effective campaign or hardest effective copy to create with a story based promotion. Your prospect has to be able to, first of all, it has to be believable. It has to be interesting. It has to be compelling. It has to lead to the establishment of certain beliefs, thoughts, recognition. The prospect has to be able to see themselves in the story. Right. It takes a lot of skill to tell a good story the right way in marketing. That's why you're going to see when I show you in just a second, that's, I have a go to lead structure, the way that I open up almost all of our campaigns, not all, but the majority, especially front end campaigns, and it is not story based and it is not fear based. And so I'm going to show you in in just a bit. So here I'm going to go through this quickly just because we're going to talk a little bit about this more tomorrow tactically, but you know you've got to recognize that remember when I said like with, you know, in in copy, and this is right like this a lot of stuff today, told you this is a lot of this a lot of stuff, and I'm still really. Just passed about the 55% mark of the slides for today. And so a lot, a lot of a lot, and you don't need to like you don't need to remember all this so I have no fear, but remember when I said that there are, you know, there are when it comes to the offer what is the prospect need to give, and what do they get right well there are perceived risks that prospects have when buying. It's not just financial loss. There are risks associated with the loss of face reputation identity character. There's loss of time. There's inconvenience work effort energy. There's the potential for hassle or intimidation. Are you now going to call me. Are you going to. Are you going to send stuff to me in the mail. Are you going to sell my information to somebody to somebody else. There's of course of course the risk of unsatisfactory results, meaning like right I won't get what was. I promise, and even though there might be a guarantee, excuse me a guarantee of the guarantee is typically financial and so it doesn't cover the loss of time it doesn't cover the loss of face it doesn't cover the potential hassle or intimidation it doesn't cover the inconvenience the work the energy the effort, it just replaces potentially replaces the the financial. The financial aspect, and so there's the fear of risk of unsatisfactory results, and then there is disappointment and frustration disappointment and frustration disappointment with the fact that the problem still exists right so I tried your your your thing I spent money on it there's potential financial loss there depending on the guarantee. There is this like I'm embarrassed I look like an idiot in front of my spouse. I wasted hours every week over the last handful of weeks man this was a pain in the butt I had to go out and buy certain tools and blah blah blah it'll learn XYZ right like I haven't gotten any any any sales calls from you but I'm afraid I may. I didn't get the results that I wanted and now I'm pissed because I'm right back in the same situation I was a month ago. These are things that you got to be aware of right because we are going to show or demonstrate. We're going to alleviate as much of these as we possibly can. We want to lessen these as much as we can right like and so we want to we want to lessen the fear of loss of face we want to we want to show the opposite we want to show the insignificant time we want to. Right show the ease of of of implementing we want to show them that there is no like no reason to be intimidated by this process by this this this opportunity and so these are things that you've got to recognize because it's not just the financial member it's not what stops people from buying isn't just the financial like so understand that. Right now I want to show you want to break this down those of you that have been through e five you've heard this just pay attention. I want to go through this quickly for everybody else. This is. So I said that there is one single belief that I always want prospects to have before I go into the offer. The aim of every marketing campaign that I create the body of the campaign is to establish that one belief. Everything that I do and say and show and present and demonstrate is there to do one thing and one thing only, and that is established that one buying belief in the mind of the prospect. Like I want you to think about it like this. I want you to recognize gang that that. And I said this before and I'll say it again, and take this to heart ponder this after the workshop ponder this tonight as you're resting up for tomorrow. And that is this that I do not when I sit down to craft a marketing campaign. I don't think like a salesperson at all. I'm not a salesperson. I don't think like a salesperson. I don't think like even like a marketer. I don't even. I don't think like a copywriter. I think more like a prosecutor and a prosecutor who is presenting a case to a jury and the prosecutor wants that case to be an airtight emotional and logical case that leads the jury to one single conclusion. Every prosecutor, they are there. They're presenting a case, and they're ultimately leading the jury to, let's say to one belief to the belief that the defendant is guilty of the crime and should be punished to the full extent of the law. And so everything that the prosecutor does and says and shows and presents and demonstrates in some way, shape or form is leading the jury methodically one piece at a time one step at a time closer and closer to that one conclusion. Right. They're not there. The good prosecutors are not going off on these meandering paths. All they're doing is just stacking belief on top of belief evidence on top of evidence that leads the jury to see. Yep, the defendant is guilty and should be punished to the full extent of the law. And it's only when the prosecutor has led the jury to that conclusion to the conclusion that the defendant is guilty and should be punished to the full extent of the law that the prosecutor then gives their closing arguments. And their call to action go into the deliberation room and find this this defendant guilty. Right. And so that's the same exact thing that I'm doing. That's the same exact thing that I am doing. Right. I'm talking to our jury. Right. Our jury is our prospects are our audience. There is one belief that I want that audience to have before I go into my closing arguments my offer. And before I ask them before I give them the call to action. Excuse me to buy and everything that I do and say and show and present is there to establish that one belief that buying belief just like the prosecutor and I'm trying to present an airtight rock solid emotional and logical argument. And so what I want to share with you now is how you formulate that buying belief. What is that buying belief that you want to lead prospects to accept before your offer. What is it that you're trying to establish in the mind of the prospect the one overarching buying belief. Understand that just like with the prosecutor prosecutor is is ultimately their ultimate aim. The ultimate belief is that the defendant is guilty and should be punished to the full extent of the law. That is the ultimate belief. The one buying belief if you will that they are trying to create in the mind of the jury. But along the way there are other sub beliefs that they're going to have to establish maybe they're going to have to establish the belief that the defendant had a motive. And so they're going to claim the defendant had a motive and then they're going to offer proof evidence support of that. Then they're going to say the defendant was right so then they're going to establish in the mind of the jury the defendant had a motive. Okay. Next thing they may be there to establish the fact that the defendant had a violent tendency. Right. And so they're going to claim that the defendant had violent tendency and then they're going to show proof evidence support of that. Then they established that belief and they might go methodically one step at a time right. The defendant was at the scene of the crime. Yep. I believe it. The defendant had a violent tendency. Yep. I believe it. The defendant was seen at the was at the scene of the crime. Yep. I believe it. Boom. Boom. Boom. Check. Check. Check. So I want to show you how you how you do that. Excuse me how you formulate that simple. There are three pieces right this is there there's a lot of different ways to to potentially do this. This is the approach that I use is the approach that I train our clients on there are three pieces to the campaign thesis. Number one is statement of superiority right statement of superiority. We're going to claim superiority in some area for the for the mechanism. So we're going to we're basically going to claim that the best way the fastest way the easiest way the most consistent way the safest way the cheapest way whatever it may be which we're going to talk about in just a second to experience the primary promise is with the unique mechanism is with this framework this system this approach this methodology right that's this is ultimately how we're forming this thesis this single statement that we're going to use as our north star as our guiding light so I do this at the beginning to identify what is the one belief that I need my jury to have by the end of my case. What is the one belief that I need my prospects to have before I introduce the offer. I'm going to write it out as a campaign thesis not copy. It's what I'm going to use right as my guiding light now when I am writing copy when I'm writing the copy and we're using all the tactics and triggers and techniques and gambits and things that we're going to talk about the language patterns tomorrow. It's all steering towards that north star along the way I'm prodding emotions I'm establishing certain beliefs right I'm offering proof I'm making claims I'm stating benefits I'm giving contrast right all leading to this one north star, which is the acceptance of this campaign thesis everything that I'm doing right and why I'm doing it in some form or fashion is leading the the prospect to the acceptance of this one statement just like the process of the process of the process of the process. I'm not sure what the future is is is doing. And so we've got the primary promise which you've already talked about. We've got the unique mechanism, right which we already talked about. I want to talk about the statement of superiority. And so this is rather simple but there are some nuances to this. So number one you've got to claim superiority in some aspect in something right if you it's either. You've got to claim superiority in terms of speed in terms of ease in terms of consistency reliability in terms of cost. In terms of effectiveness, you've got to claim superiority in something. How do you claim superiority in one or more of those areas and that comes from your interrogation. That comes from your examination right you're going to know like it. This is the best because it's the easiest this is the fastest is the most effective is this the most consistent is the most reliable. What is it might be one of those it might be two of those right. But you've got to identify if it's if it's superior in terms of speed then it's the fastest. The fastest has to be superior in terms of speed if it wasn't superior in terms of speed then it couldn't be the fastest. It's the fastest be fast wouldn't be the fastest. It's the fastest. That means it's superior in terms of speed. If it's the easiest. That means it's superior in terms of ease. If it's the most reliable that means it's your claiming superiority in reliability right if it's the safest right that means you're claiming superiority in terms of safety if it's the cheapest. That means that you are claiming superiority in terms of of cost right and so you have to identify what is it that you are claiming superiority for and then it's just a matter of claiming it in this in this campaign thesis. You have to understand one one other subtle point that you can certainly have more than one so you can have the the fastest and easiest way to lose 30 pounds from your waist in the next 10 weeks right like you could have something like that the easiest and fastest but keep in mind that whatever it is that you are claiming in terms of superiority in here we have to then prove and establish in the body of your copy. In the body of your copy and so you have to understand that if you say it's the easiest fastest and most effective way to blah blah blah blah see understanding that claiming superiority taking those things is not just a matter of what would they be most excited about. Well they'd be most excited about all right they'd be most excited about it's the fastest easiest most effective way to safely change white like the safest way to blah blah blah they'd be most they'd be most excited about all of those. But it makes your job that much more difficult because now you have to prove why is it the fastest why is it the easiest why is it the most effective why is it the safest you have to prove all that remember this is this campaign thesis. This is the belief that we are going to establish in the mind of the prospect before we present the offer. And so if you claim that it's the fastest easiest and most effective will then like in order to establish that belief you have to show why what makes it the fastest easiest and most effective. You got to show what makes it the fastest what makes it the easiest what makes it the most effective. And so my point is that the more of these aspects of superiority you choose the longer the campaign is going to be the more things you're going to have to prove. And so you only want to claim superiority in the areas that you are confident you can prove don't claim something don't claim superiority in an area that you can't prove. And so. So G said is it better to stick to one claim of superiority. Not necessarily. No. I would say that it is better to stick to the claims of superiority in the areas that you feel overconfident you can prove right and so the ones where you're like I could definitely prove this I can I can show this like I can explain why right and I'm going to show you a simple way to offer proof in just a second and then you're going to see how this is carried over tomorrow into copy. going to skip ahead and so let's do this all right so check this out I want to talk to you about the lead now we're not going to go through the lead is obviously as you know the first 350 400 500 words I'm not going to go through all the different lead that we're going to do because what I'm going to show you or give you is my go to lead approach there's a lot of different things that you can do in terms of the different lead type like from direct to indirect, you know, story to offer like there's a lot of different things that you can do in terms of the different lead type to show you my go to on the front end and I want to point out a couple of of key things. So when we're talking about the lead here what we are talking about is we we're talking about the lead type of the lead type of the lead type of the lead type of the lead type is my go to lead approach there's a lot of different things that you can do in terms of the different lead type of like from direct to indirect you know story to offer like there's a lot of different lead types reality is I'm going to show you my go to Ministeri Fyr dead y head looks to justify what the heart wants. Right? The head looks to justify what the heart wants. And so we always in marketing speak to the heart first. We always quote unquote sell to or market to or talk to or convince, persuade the heart first because the head will look for justification for what the heart wants. And so the number one objective of the lead, the opening of your campaign is to set the emotional hook is to set the emotional hook. What I mean by set the emotional hook is get your prospect emotionally excited and they get emotionally excited by the idea of a different way to give them the result that they want. Don't overthink it. Do not overcomplicate it. When I say set the emotional hook, we're talking about getting your prospect emotionally excited, emotionally sold, emotionally bought in, emotionally bought in. I want them to say this sounds amazing. Now prove to me that will work and prove to me that will work for me. That's ultimately what I want the prospect to say to themselves or to be thinking at the end of our lead. This sounds amazing. Like this sounds killer. This sounds off the charts. Like if this is legit sold, but prove to me that it will work, man, prove to me that it will work for me. That's the type of thinking. Right. And so that doesn't come about from a logical, rational argument. That comes about from what I would call and I think it was. It might have been Michael Drummond actually on here who shared it this way. That comes about from giving, presenting what I would call what he would call what we now call the elevator pitch for your campaign or really the elevator pitch for your mechanism and the payoff. Right. For this, I've got a different thing and here's what it'll do for you and here's what it'll do for you and here's what it'll do for you. Right. Like all we are doing is teasing the most exciting benefits of the mechanism. We're not giving an argument. We're not giving rationale. We're not explaining how it works. Right. We're taking the most exciting stuff about the mechanism and what it does. Remember the most exciting stuff about the mechanism, the most exciting stuff in general, is always about what it will do for the prospect. We're taking the most exciting stuff about the mechanism. Right. And we're bringing two, three, four of them up front and we're leading with that. We're promising something big. We're promising a transformation. We're teasing more. Right. We're telling them what it doesn't require of them. Right. And you'll see this in just a second, like a real example of this. And ultimately, at the end of the lead, like I said, I just want the prospect to say, wow, this sounds amazing. It sounds amazing. Now prove to me that it'll work and prove to me that it'll work for me. That's it. That's all I, I, I, I, I, I, we are trying to do. Nothing more, nothing less. I'm not trying to convince. I'm not trying to prove. I'm not trying to establish the campaign thesis. I'm not trying to do any of that. I just want them emotionally like this sounds amazing. Oh my gosh, this sounds like right. They're not, they're not convinced. They're not convinced. They're not sold, but they're, they're emotionally bought in. Right. Like the very first thing that we got to do is get them to emotionally buy in. And what I like to say, the analogy that I've used for years is the reason why I say it as setting the emotional hook is because if you've ever gone fishing, you know, I've told, I've told the story before, but you know, like fishing, like when you feel, you know, when, when if you go freshwater fishing and you feel, oh man, there's a, you know, there's a bass on the end of this bad boy or whatnot and you just start reeling in. What ends up happening, which happened to me many times when I first, right? You start reeling in and you start reeling in and all of a sudden the line goes slack and the fish lets the, the, the bait, the lure out of its mouth. Why? Because before you start reeling in, you've got to set the hook. You got to set the hook, set the hook and then you reel in. You set the hook, then reel in. You don't reel in before setting the hook. Reeling in in our context is, is presenting the argument, is giving the logical, rational support. You don't start reeling in. You don't start giving the evidence. You don't start giving the rationale. You don't start giving the logical support. You don't start delivering those elements until you set that emotional hook, until you get them excited emotionally about what this can mean and do for them. Wow, this sounds awesome. Now proof to me that it will work and proof to me that it will work for me. Now a simple way to think about this, right? And if we talked about nothing else, you could go legitimately, you can go and do this because there's no, there's no real science to the, to the lead per se, right? Teasing the most exciting benefits, right? Of the mechanism, but a copyright or a copy chief from Agora by the name of Kyle Milligan. I got to give credit where where credit is due. I think he articulated what you want to cover in the lead. He talks about this in the context of the entire campaign, but this is really most appropriate for you guys in the lead. He summed it up like this, what you want to, what you want to convey or what you want to tap into with the teasing of the benefits up front is new, easy, safe and big. And what he's really talking about here is right like this is a new and different way. This is new and different way from what it is that you've tried before. So we're immediately answering the question, what's different about this? Right? A new and different way to to to experience the outcome. What we've already talked about. He also said right that secondary to that is only that is when you have a like if you have a an unaddressed root, you know, root problem, you know, like if you have a right, like this is the only way to do that. This is the only way to take advantage of XYZ. This is the only way to solve elemento pay so it could be either either or. I tend to look for new and different, right? New and different, it's got to be in the in the lead. Next is easy, all right? Easy, another version of that, the kind of the sister to that is anybody, anybody could do this. And so right, those two are not necessarily are not necessarily the same, right? But oftentimes, right? Like I might not be able to say that that E5 method is easy because it's not easy and so I'm not going to say that it's easy if it's not easy. But it's certainly something that anybody can do, right? Any marketer can use this. Any marketer willing to willing to put in the effort can reap the rewards of this. And so you want some form or fashion of either easy or anybody can do this in the lead, right? Safe and predictable, meaning that this isn't a this isn't a fly by night thing like this is producing results. You can bank on this, you can rely on this, you can expect that. This is this is going to continue to work for you safe in their world comes from. You know, it comes from the financial investing or financial publishing background and so that's more safe investment. This is more about this is a reliable process that will produce consistent consistent results for you. It's not like a maybe it's not an if. In other words, you can feel confident that you will experience success from this. And then the last one is big and fast. And I would say that it's either a big result, big outcome, big transformation, big change, right? Or or you talk about speed, right? Like how quickly you can experience results. And so this idea of new, easy, safe and big, when I go back to this, when I go back to this bad boy over here, think of it as your elevator pitch, teasing out the most exciting benefits. And that's all we're doing in the lead. We're not we're not explaining like we're not getting into into the argument portion. We're not getting into the logical portion. All we're doing is setting the emotional hook and we're leveraging in some capacity these big four feelings, right? Or a mix of these. Now, I'm going to show you an example of a campaign that I wrote that lives this out, that lives and breathes this. I'll show you one example. I'll probably show you another example tomorrow just to drive home this point. But before I do, I want to make one additional point, because you're going to see this in the example that I'm about to show you. This is a quote from Winston Churchill and it says, if you have an important point to make, don't try to be subtle or clever. Use a pile driver, hit the point once, then come back and hit it again, then hit it a third time, a tremendous whack. The reason why I shared this in here is because what you're going to find in your lead, really in your campaigns in general, and we're going to cover this tomorrow. I'm going to remind you of this tomorrow, but we have the idea of repetition. Repetition, repetition is good. Being repetitious is not. So being repetitious, I'm going to talk about tomorrow, because that's word choice and repetition is good. What am I saying here? I'm saying that you want to continue to drive home the main point, the main promise, the main outcome, the main idea over and over and over and over and over and over. Don't think just because you said it once that that's enough and that they're going to get the point, you drive it home over and over and over. The key is that we want to leverage repetition so that we never veer too far away from the thing that is most exciting to them, but we don't want to be repetitious. Repetitious comes from saying the same thing over and over. Repetition means we're driving home the same idea. Being repetitious means we're driving home the same idea with the same words. And so we can drive home the same idea without being repetitious by changing the word, the words that we use. And so what I want to show you is I want to show you an example. If you're not going to go with my go to, which is you can see it's not a story lead, nor is it problem solution lead when you use a problem solution. When you start with the problem, when you're dealing with unresolved, you know, distress, when you're starting with pain and you're going to write, you start with the problem, you agitate the problem and then you get this to get to the solution. Don't labor on the problem. In other words, the mistake that I see folks make when they use a problem solution lead is they stay on the problem way too long. Hit the problem, agitate the problem, meaning show the prospect where the problem is going to lead them if they do not resolve it or do not take care of it, take care of it properly and then get to the solution. The hope comes from the solution. The prospect already knows the problem that they have. You just want to let them know that you know it. You want to agitate it a little bit, show them that, right? If they don't fix it, what could happen? And then you want to get to the solution. So just because it's problem, agitate and then solve, doesn't mean that it's 30, you know, 33, 33, 33 percent. You want to hit the problem fast, agitate it fast, get to the solution in the bulk of your time, you want to be focused on the on the solution. And so with that, let me do this. And so Jan said, Todd, how would this work if I would apply this on a landing page for an opt-in to a VSL webinar or resource for someone that is going to book a call, booking a call? Would I still use a lead and the proof with the bullet points? So no. So what I want you to think about what I what I what I want you to understand is that's a bit of of strategy Jan. And so think about this. Every page has its purpose. Every page serves a role. The page of an opt-in or registration is to get the the qualified opt-in or registration. In other words, we're not just trying to get the email opt-in for the sake of getting the email opt-in. We want folks, we want the right folks to opt-in and we want those folks to know what it is that they're opting in for. And we want those folks to opt in because they want the thing that we're talking about. And so the opt-in page is just about really presenting the the the most part. This isn't and every every industry is a little different depending on what's out there, but is the promise and the mechanism like the reality is I love ultra streamlined lead gen pages. They can be ultra streamlined when you're able to make a when you're able to like you're able to communicate your your hook, your promise and your mechanism in a single headline. The reality is you don't need a whole bunch of of copy when the idea is right when the promise is right and when there's a mechanism behind there. You don't need a whole bunch of copy. Most of the time I see honestly, the more copy that gets added to an opt-in page, the more likely there is to be copy that talks the prospect out of opting in. The more likely there is to be copy that makes the prospect say, oh, that. Oh, I know what they're talking about. Oh, I've heard that. Right. And we're going to talk about that tomorrow when we talk about avoiding the categorical imperative how like you want to avoid mental opt out. And so you want to prevent that mental opt out, meaning you don't want prospects to feel like I've seen this. I've heard this. And so the likelihood is the more copy that you add to a page, the more likely you are to say something that's going to talk a prospect out of opting in. We're talking about the opt-in page. And so I'm a big fan of going with streamlined, streamlined opt-in pages. Oftentimes with a headline complex and a call to action button, honestly. And so there you go.
